title: Victoria sponge with buttercream
subtitle: Classic equal-weight sponge with jam and vanilla buttercream
excerpt: The Victoria sponge uses equal weights of butter, sugar, eggs, and flour. It is assembled cold with raspberry jam and vanilla buttercream rather than cream, which makes it stable enough to stand at room temperature for a day.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136 — the Victoria sandwich cake appears in the cakes chapter, named after Queen Victoria who was said to enjoy slices of the cake with afternoon tea. The equal-weight formula (butter:sugar:eggs:flour) is the definitive British sponge method.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the sponge layers uniced, wrapped individually. Defrost at room temperature and assemble before serving.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake the sponge layers the day before, cool completely, and store in an airtight box. Assemble with jam and buttercream on the day of serving.

glossaryTerms.definition: The technique of beating butter and sugar together until pale, fluffy, and significantly increased in volume before adding eggs and flour. The process incorporates air into the fat, which expands in the oven and gives sponge cakes their rise. The butter must be genuinely soft (not cold) for the air to be trapped efficiently.

content.text: The Victoria sponge is built on the

: butter and sugar beaten together until pale and fluffy, then eggs added one at a time, then flour folded in. The equal weights of each main ingredient make it simple to scale — weigh the eggs first and use the same weight of everything else.

Heat the oven to 180°C (160°C fan). Grease two 20 cm round cake tins and line the bases with baking paper. Beat {{unsalted-butter}} and {{caster-sugar}} together for 4–5 minutes until pale and noticeably fluffy. Add {{vanilla-extract}}, then beat in the {{eggs}} one at a time, adding a tablespoon of the measured {{self-raising-flour}} with each egg if the mixture starts to curdle. Sift in the remaining {{self-raising-flour}} and {{baking-powder}} and fold in gently with a large spoon — stop as soon as the flour disappears. Divide between the tins and smooth the tops. Bake for 20–22 minutes until risen and a skewer inserted in the centre comes out clean. The cakes should spring back when lightly pressed.

Leave the cakes in their tins for 5 minutes before turning out onto a wire rack. Leave to cool completely before filling. Beat the filling {{unsalted-butter}}, {{icing-sugar}}, and {{vanilla-extract}} until smooth. Spread the {{raspberry-jam}} over the flat side of the bottom layer, then the buttercream on top of the jam. Place the second sponge on top. Dust with {{icing-sugar}} just before serving.
